docs.title: Guides for Detecting and Addressing Bias in AI Systems
docs.text: Welcome! These playbooks provide structured workflows for identifying bias, measuring fairness, and applying interventions to AI systems. Enjoy!

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: AI systems can unintentionally reproduce biases present in data. These playbooks help teams: - Detect bias in datasets and models - Measure its impact on different user groups, including intersectional subgroups - Apply interventions to improve fairness - Maintain responsible AI practices over time

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• Fairness Audit Playbook
  • Fairness Intervention Playbook
  • Fairness Implementation Strategies

docs.title: How to Use These Playbooks
docs.text:
• Start with the Audit Playbook to detect bias
  • Move to the Intervention Playbook to correct it
  • Apply Implementation Strategies for long-term fairness

docs.title: 1 Causal Modeling Template
docs.text: The Causal Modeling Template provides a structured approach to mapping causal relationships that may create unfairness in AI systems. It helps users identify key variables, their relationships, and points for intervention.

docs.title: Protected Attribute Identification
docs.text:
1. Primary protected attributes: [List legally protected characteristics relevant to this application]
  2. Intersectional categories: [List relevant combinations of protected attributes, e.g., gender × race, age × disability] Guidance : Include multi-attribute combinations in causal graphs to capture intersectional bias.

docs.title: Mediator Variable Identification
docs.text:
1. Variables directly influenced by protected attributes: [List variables]
  2. Evidence for causal relationship: [Brief justification per variable] Guidance: Use domain knowledge, historical data, or prior studies to justify causal assumptions quantitatively.

docs.title: Proxy Variable Identification
docs.text:
1. Variables correlated with protected attributes: [List variables]
  2. Evidence for correlation: [Brief justification per variable]
  3. Common causes explaining correlation: [Explanation per variable]

docs.title: Outcome Variable Identification
docs.text:
1. Decisions or predictions made by system: [List outcomes]
  2. Evaluation metrics used: [List metrics] Guidance: Include fairness evaluation metrics such as demographic parity difference, equalized odds, and path-specific fairness measures.

docs.title: Legitimate Predictor Identification
docs.text:
1. Variables that should influence outcomes: [List variables]
  2. Justification for legitimacy: [Brief justification per variable]

docs.title: Causal Graph Construction Guidelines
docs.text:
• Use directed arrows for causal relationships.
  • Use bidirectional dashed arrows for correlations without direct causation.
  • Distinguish protected attributes, mediators, proxies, and outcomes using different node shapes.
  • Document causal assumptions for each arrow.
  • Identify critical paths transmitting potential discrimination.
  • Provide example causal graph diagrams for guidance.

docs.title: 2 Counterfactual Analysis Framework
docs.text: The Counterfactual Analysis Framework evaluates whether the AI system exhibits causal discrimination by analyzing predictions under hypothetical changes to protected attributes.

docs.title: Counterfactual Query Structure
docs.text:
1. Base case description:
  2. Individual characteristics: [Relevant non-protected attributes]
  3. Protected attribute value: [Current value]

  4. System prediction: [Current prediction/decision]

  5. Counterfactual scenario:

  6. Modified protected attribute: [Counterfactual value]
  7. Variables to remain constant: [List causally independent variables]

  8. Variables to change: [List descendants of protected attributes]

  9. Fairness evaluation:

  10. Expected outcome under counterfactual: [Prediction if fair]
  11. Actual model behavior: [What model actually does]
  12. Discrepancy analysis: [Compare expected vs. actual]
  13. Include path-specific quantitative measures:
  14. Natural Direct Effect (NDE) – Effect of protected attribute on outcome not through mediators
  15. Natural Indirect Effect (NIE) – Effect transmitted through mediator variables
  16. Compare NDE and NIE across intersectional subgroups for deeper analysis.

docs.title: Path-Specific Effect Analysis
docs.text:
• Identify specific causal pathways from protected attributes to outcomes.
  • Classify paths as legitimate or problematic using domain knowledge.
  • Quantify contribution of each path to observed disparities.
  • Focus interventions on problematic paths while preserving legitimate ones.
  • Use tables or diagrams to visualize each path’s contribution to disparities.

docs.title: 3 Intervention Point Identification Method
docs.text: The Intervention Point Identification Method determines where in the ML pipeline to intervene based on causal analysis. It maps causal patterns to appropriate intervention types.

docs.title: Intervention Selection Decision Tree
docs.text:
1. If direct discrimination is present (direct path from protected attribute to outcome): a. Is the protected attribute explicitly used as a feature?

    • Yes: Apply in-processing constraints or remove the attribute
    • No: Investigate implicit direct discrimination through model architecture

  2. If proxy discrimination is present (path through correlated but not causally related variables): a. Can the proxy variables be identified?

    • Yes: Consider pre-processing approaches to transform these variables
    • No: Apply in-processing regularization to minimize proxy use

  3. If mediator discrimination is present (path through variables causally influenced by protected attributes): a. Are the mediator variables legitimate predictors for the task?

    • Yes: Consider using multi-objective optimization to balance fairness with prediction
    • No: Apply pre-processing to remove the influence of protected attributes on these variables

  4. If outcome discrimination is present (disparities in model outputs): a. Are the disparities consistent across subgroups?

    • Yes: Consider post-processing approaches like threshold optimization
    • No: Apply more targeted interventions based on causal structure

docs.title: Limited Information Adaptation Guidelines:
docs.text: When causal structure is uncertain, test multiple plausible models. Perform sensitivity analysis to identify robust intervention decisions. Prioritize resolving uncertainties that would change intervention recommendations. Document assumptions explicitly to enable future refinement.

docs.title: 4 Integration and Validation Guidance
docs.text:
Map findings from causal analysis to pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing interventions.

Define quantitative validation metrics for each intervention:

Change in demographic parity, equalized odds, path-specific fairness metrics

Impact on overall model performance (accuracy, RMSE, AUC, etc.)

Include intersectional subgroup analysis in validation reports.

Establish a monitoring plan for post-deployment bias drift.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
AI systems across the bank support critical decisions — from loan approvals to fraud detection and customer risk profiling. However, recent internal reviews have revealed growing concerns about fairness. In particular, disparities in outcomes across gender and other demographic groups have highlighted a major organizational gap:

While fairness assessments exist, fairness interventions do not.

Different engineering teams currently respond to fairness issues using inconsistent and ad hoc solutions. This creates:

  • uneven mitigation approaches
  • duplicated effort
  • unclear accountability
  • risk of systemic bias persisting across systems

To address this, we are introducing the Fairness Intervention Playbook — a standardized methodology that enables engineering teams to move from detecting bias to actively mitigating it.

This playbook operationalizes fairness by transforming isolated tools into a coordinated intervention strategy that can be used across:

  • internally built AI systems
  • vendor APIs
  • legacy ML models

Most importantly, it enables teams to act independently in common cases while still supporting escalation to fairness experts when needed.

docs.title: Why This Playbook Exists
docs.text:
Historically, fairness work inside the organization has focused on:

  • measuring disparities
  • auditing model outcomes

But measurement alone does not change outcomes.

Engineering teams require:

  • clear intervention pathways
  • structured decision points
  • repeatable workflows
  • alignment between fairness goals and business impact

Over time, several practical intervention tools were developed while supporting product teams:

docs.title: Existing Internal Toolkits
docs.text:
• Causal Fairness Toolkit — identifies how protected attributes influence downstream variables like employment history or income
  • Pre-Processing Fairness Toolkit — improves representation in training data
  • In-Processing Fairness Toolkit — embeds fairness constraints directly into model training
  • Post-Processing Fairness Toolkit — adjusts prediction thresholds to improve outcome parity

Individually, these tools helped in isolated cases. Collectively, they form the foundation of a scalable intervention methodology.

docs.title: From Toolkits to Methodology
docs.text:
The Fairness Intervention Playbook brings these components together into a single structured workflow.

Rather than applying fairness fixes reactively, the playbook enables teams to:

  1. Identify where bias enters the system
  2. Select the most effective intervention layer
  3. Apply coordinated mitigation strategies
  4. Validate fairness improvements without compromising system performance

docs.title: Multi-Stage Intervention Pipeline
docs.text:
Each intervention layer plays a distinct role:

Intervention Layer Purpose Causal Analysis Identifies structural sources of bias and intervention points Pre-Processing Improves fairness at the data level In-Processing Enforces fairness during model training Post-Processing Adjusts outputs to reduce disparities

Each layer builds on the previous one:

  • Causal insights guide data interventions
  • Data interventions influence model training
  • Model training affects final outcomes

Together, they enable fairness to be engineered — not just evaluated.

docs.title: What This Playbook Enables
docs.text:
This playbook is designed to help teams:

  • Implement fairness interventions consistently
  • Understand trade-offs between fairness and performance
  • Address intersectional bias
  • Integrate mitigation steps into existing ML workflows
  • Validate improvements using measurable criteria

It supports:

  • classification and regression systems
  • structured and unstructured data pipelines
  • internal and third-party AI tools

And it can be applied across domains including:

  • lending
  • risk modeling
  • marketing personalization
  • customer analytics

docs.title: How to Use This Playbook
docs.text: This playbook is structured as a modular system of guides:

docs.title: Core Components
docs.text:
• Causal Fairness Methodology
  • Pre-Processing Intervention Guide
  • In-Processing Intervention Guide
  • Post-Processing Intervention Guide

docs.title: Supporting Components
docs.text:
• Workflow Integration Guide
  • Case Study
  • Validation Framework

Engineering teams can:

  • apply individual components when needed
  • or follow the full workflow for high-risk systems

Most use cases will not require fairness specialists — making fairness intervention operational rather than theoretical.
